mnemonics: CUBS: Structures are like Custom Uniform Building Sets, allowing you to combine different data types into a single unit. Unions are like Universal Building Sets, where all pieces share the same space, and only one type is used at a time.
keyPoints: A structure (struct) groups variables of different types under a single name, with each member having its own memory space.
A union shares the same memory location for all its members; only one member can contain a value at any given time.
Unions are memory-efficient for scenarios where only one type of data is needed at a time, while structures are used for grouping related data of different types.
commonMistakes: Accessing a union member that was not the most recently written to, leading to undefined behavior.
Assuming structure members are stored in memory in the exact order declared, which can be affected by padding and alignment requirements.
